questions.prompt: How are the lessons in this course organised?
choices: Short lessons grouped into topics, with a checkpoint between each topic and a single final scenario assessment at the end.

choices.label: Weekly cohorts that move through the same lesson at the same time.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the format. The course is self-paced; there are no cohorts.

choices.label: One long course you work through end-to-end without breaks.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The course is deliberately split into topics so you can take it in sittings.

choices.label: Two tracks (EDR and ITDR) where you pick one.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Everyone covers both surfaces; the lesson sequence is shared.

questions.explanation: Topics of short lessons with a checkpoint between them, and a single final scenario assessment at the end.

questions.prompt: A peer says: "Passing the final assessment IS the sign-off to work live tickets unsupervised." Why is that the wrong reading?

choices.label: Because the assessment is too easy to count as a sign-off.
choices.rationale: Wrong framing. The issue isn't difficulty; it's what kind of input the score is.

choices: Because the final is a recommendation of readiness, not a sign-off. Your MSP has its own shadow-and-review process for unsupervised work.

choices.label: Because Huntress runs the assessment and your MSP isn't involved.
choices.rationale: The training assessment is the training's, not Huntress-the-vendor's. The split that matters is MSP vs. LMS.

choices.label: Because the score isn't recorded anywhere.
choices.rationale: The score is recorded; that's how the LMS gates. What it means is the question.

questions.explanation: The LMS says you can make the calls; your MSP's review says you make them well on real customers.

questions.prompt: You're partway through the course. A senior offers to look at an EDR ticket together right now. EDR triage lessons land much later in the course. What's the right move?

choices.label: Say yes and drive the ticket while the senior watches.
choices.rationale: Skips the gates the course exists to enforce. Without the platform model, the senior watches you click in the wrong places.

choices.label: Decline and finish the current topic first.
choices.rationale: Defensible but over-cautious. There's a middle path that respects the cadence and the senior's offer.

choices: Say yes, shadow rather than drive, and capture questions for after.

choices.label: Ignore the senior and keep reading the lessons in order.
choices.rationale: Wrong tone. The offer is real and worth taking; the question is how.

questions.explanation: Shadowing means you're not skipping gates; you're watching the work happen on a real ticket. Capture anything you didn't understand.

questions.prompt: What three things does a useful questions-list entry capture?
choices: Where (lesson, ticket, page), what you didn't understand in your own words, and what you tried.

choices.label: When you got stuck, who was online, and how long you stalled for.
choices.rationale: Wrong shape. These aren't actionable for the senior at a check-in.

choices.label: Lesson number, vendor URL, and a screenshot of the confusing passage.
choices.rationale: Screenshots without your own words just reproduce the confusion.

choices.label: The exact text that confused you and nothing else.
choices.rationale: Quoting alone misses your framing and what you tried, the parts that turn a question into a check-in conversation.

questions.explanation: The "what you tried" line is the line that earns the rest its keep.

questions.prompt: Which field of a questions-list entry pulls the most weight, and why?

choices.label: "Where." Without the lesson number the senior can't look anything up.
choices.rationale: Useful but not load-bearing. The senior can usually find the lesson.

choices: "What you tried." It blocks trivial questions, gives the senior usable context, and builds the habit of trying before asking.

choices.label: "What you didn't understand." Without it the entry is meaningless.
choices.rationale: Necessary, but the lesson is explicit that "what you tried" pulls the most weight.

choices.label: The timestamp. Recent questions get answered first.
choices.rationale: Timestamps aren't a recommended field.

questions.explanation: Three benefits in one line: stops trivial questions, gives the senior context, and builds the habit of trying before asking.

questions.prompt: You're on a lesson late in the evening. It references a diagnostic you haven't seen before. No senior is available. What do you do?

choices.label: Message the on-call senior to clarify.
choices.rationale: On-call is for live incidents, not training questions. Burns goodwill and you might not get an answer until morning anyway.

choices: Write a one-line note (where, what, what you tried), and move on to the next lesson.

choices.label: Re-read the lesson three more times until you feel like you've absorbed it.
choices.rationale: Poor return on time. By the third re-read you've stopped engaging and started rationalising.

choices.label: Stop entirely and pick up tomorrow.
choices.rationale: Stalling for a day is one of the two failure modes the lesson is built to prevent.

questions.explanation: Capture-and-keep-moving is the default. The note unblocks you and surfaces at the next check-in if it doesn't resolve itself.

questions.prompt: "I'll remember the question without writing it down." The lesson treats this as a reliable habit.
questions.explanation: You'll remember the feeling of being confused at a particular spot, not the specific question. By the check-in, the senior asks what wasn't clear and you can't reconstruct it.

questions.prompt: Roughly what percentage of daily Huntress ticket volume does this course target as in scope for the helpdesk tech to handle independently?

choices.label: About 50% — the rest goes to a senior or to Huntress support.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the scope. A competent helpdesk tech is doing most of the work independently after this course.

choices: About 95% — the remaining 5% (Critical investigation, detection tuning, commercial decisions, irreversible policy changes) always escalates.

choices.label: 100% — once trained, the tech handles everything.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the ceiling. The remaining 5% escalates regardless of how trained you are.

choices.label: About 25% — the helpdesk only does triage; everything else escalates.
choices.rationale: Understates the role and inverts the ceiling intent.

questions.explanation: Roughly 95% in scope; the remaining 5% (Critical investigation, detection tuning, commercial decisions, irreversible policy changes) always escalates.

questions.prompt: Which of these is in scope (the "you do" column) for the helpdesk tech this course targets?

choices.label: Modify org-level isolation policy.
choices.rationale: Right-hand column — escalates. Policy-level changes are above the helpdesk ceiling.

choices: Recognise a Critical incident, contain (isolate), then escalate in parallel.

choices.label: Investigate a Critical incident and hunt persistence.
choices.rationale: Right-hand column — escalates. Forensics is above the helpdesk ceiling.

choices.label: Suppress or tune detections at the policy level.
choices.rationale: Right-hand column — escalates. Detection tuning is above the ceiling.

questions.explanation: Contain-and-escalate-in-parallel is in scope; investigation of a Critical isn't.

questions.prompt: The course previews a "two-question test" that holds the escalation ceiling together. What does it ask?
choices: Is this reversible, and do I understand why it's the right action? If either answer is no, escalate.

choices.label: Is this Critical, and has the SOC closed the incident? If yes to both, proceed.
choices.rationale: Severity isn't the two-question test; it's the tempo input. Reversibility and understanding are the ceiling test.

choices.label: Will the customer notice, and will my senior approve? If yes to both, proceed.
choices.rationale: Misframes the test as social — actually a technical reversibility plus understanding check.

choices.label: Did the SOC sign the report, and is my MSP licensed for the action? If yes, proceed.
choices.rationale: Not the test.

questions.explanation: Reversibility plus understanding. If either answer is no, escalate.

questions.prompt: A client calls: their server is locked up, ransomware-shaped. The portal shows only a Low EDR detection with the SOC's recommendation still pending. What do you do first?

choices.label: Approve the pending recommendation so the SOC sees it move.
choices.rationale: Pending means the SOC analyst hasn't finished triage; approving a recommendation that doesn't exist yet skips them.

choices: Isolate the host and escalate to a senior in parallel.

choices.label: Tell the client you can't act because the SOC hasn't completed triage.
choices.rationale: The client gave you new information the SOC doesn't have yet. Act on containment and feed the new context to both the SOC and the senior.

choices.label: Wait for the SOC to upgrade the severity, then act.
choices.rationale: The Low tag is stale relative to the call. Waiting on a re-grade while the customer is locked up is the freeze failure mode.

questions.explanation: The client's description overrides the stale Low tag. Contain (isolate), escalate in parallel, feed new context to the SOC via the ticket.

questions.prompt: Mid-incident, your senior says: "Disable scheduled-task remediations across this customer's whole org for the next 24 hours." Org-level policy changes sit in the escalate column. What do you do?

choices.label: Do it — the senior is the authority.
choices.rationale: Modifying org-level policy doesn't come down even when a senior asks. Clarify before acting.

choices: Pause and ask the senior to either run the change themselves or document why a tech is being asked to do it.

choices.label: Refuse, push back, and lecture the senior about the ceiling table.
choices.rationale: Lecturing mid-incident is wrong tone. The same outcome without the friction is the goal.

choices.label: Make the change and document the senior's request in the ticket.
choices.rationale: Documenting an above-ceiling action doesn't bring it back into scope.

questions.explanation: Not refusing; flagging that this is above the tech's line and asking for the right authority to either own it or sign off in writing.
